title: The Real Function of Technology: Marginal Productivity, Not Labor Replacement

source.original: Marc Andreessen: Will a16z Go Public & Why Labour Displacement with AI is Wrong (20VC)

body_html:
Andreessen draws a clean line between what he calls "classical economics" and "Marxist analysis" in understanding technology's relationship to labor. The Marxist view: there's a fixed amount of work, and machines take it from humans. The classical view: technology's function is to raise the marginal productivity of the individual worker.

The progression is concrete. You write on pencil and paper; you get a typewriter — more productive. You have a typewriter; you get a word processor — more productive. You do accounting by hand; you get a spreadsheet — more productive. You manage social media manually; you get AI tools — more productive. At each stage, the technology doesn't eliminate the worker. It makes the worker capable of producing more value per hour.

The critical observation is what happens to work hours when productivity tools arrive. Andreessen asks about people who code with AI: "Are they working more or fewer hours?" The answer: more. When a tool makes you 10x more productive, you don't do the same work in 1/10 the time and go home. You do 10x more work, because the expanded capability opens possibilities that were previously impractical. The coder who can now build in a day what used to take a month doesn't stop after one day — they build 30 things in a month, each spawning new requirements and opportunities.

This is the demand creation mechanism applied at the individual level. Macro-economically, technology creates new demand by lowering prices and raising real incomes. Micro-economically, technology creates new demand by expanding what an individual can accomplish, which reveals further work that needs doing. Both mechanisms produce the same outcome: more total work, not less, despite — because of — greater productivity per worker.
